D3 Chemistry L6 Create Reference

ECL — Light from Electrodes, No External Lamp

an electrode oxidises [Ru(bipy)₃]²⁺ and reduces a coreactant; their electron-transfer recombination lands Ru in S₁, which emits the same 620 nm orange band

Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) is light from a redox reaction at an electrode. The classic system is [Ru(bipy)₃]²⁺ in tripropylamine (TPA) buffer. Applying ~+1.2 V at the electrode oxidises both Ru²⁺→Ru³⁺ and TPA→TPA•⁺. TPA•⁺ loses a proton to TPA•, a strong reductant that hands one electron back to Ru³⁺ landing it in the excited [Ru(bipy)₃]²⁺* singlet. Decay emits 620 nm orange light. No external lamp needed — used in clinical immunoassays (Elecsys).

Drag the applied potential. Below the Ru²⁺/³⁺ threshold (~+1.05 V) nothing happens; above it, output rises sharply; further oxidation degrades TPA and quenches. The window shows the orange ECL glow at the active potential.
